questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A conservative net force Fx is exerted on an object within a system. The force varies as a function of the position x of the object, as shown in the graph. There are no net external forces exerted on the system. Which of the following correctly describes the potential energy Usys of the system as the object moves from 0m to 0.10m?

optionList.optionContent: Usys increases only.
Usys decreases only.
Usys decreases and then increases.
Usys does not change.

questionOption:
A .

Usys increases only.

B .

Usys decreases only.

C .

Usys decreases and then increases.

D .

Usys does not change.

questionList.questionTitle: 1D kinematics - v-t graph from motion diagram
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A constant force is exerted on a block, causing the block to move across a horizontal surface in the positive x-direction. There is nonnegligible friction between the block and the horizontal surface. The diagram shows the position of the block at 0.5s time intervals. Which of the following provides a velocity-time graph that could correspond to the diagram and provides correct reasoning?

Velocity-Time GraphReasoning
AThe distance traveled by the block during each half-second interval increases, so the velocity of the block must increase.
BThe block is traveling in the positive x-direction, so the velocity graph must have a positive slope.
CThe force of friction exerted on the block causes the acceleration of the block to decrease.
DThe block reaches a constant speed.

optionList.optionContent: A
B
C
D

questionOption:
A .

A

B .

B

C .

C

D .

D

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Two disks, A and B, rotate for 4 seconds. Each of their angular velocities, ω, are measured over time and graphed in the figure. Which of the following correctly compares the angular displacements ΔθA and ΔθB of disks A and B, respectively, during the period shown, and provides a correct reasoning?

optionList.optionContent: ΔθA=ΔθB because Disk A has a greater angular velocity at the end of the time interval shown.
ΔθA=ΔθB because neither curve has a decreasing slope.
ΔθA≠ΔθB because Disk B stops rotating after 1 second.
ΔθA≠ΔθB because the area under Disk A’s curve is greater than the area under Disk B’s curve.

questionOption:
A .

ΔθA=ΔθB because Disk A has a greater angular velocity at the end of the time interval shown.

B .

ΔθA=ΔθB because neither curve has a decreasing slope.

C .

ΔθA≠ΔθB because Disk B stops rotating after 1 second.

D .

ΔθA≠ΔθB because the area under Disk A’s curve is greater than the area under Disk B’s curve.

questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A physical pendulum is constructed of a small block of mass 2m attached to the end of a uniform rod of length ℓ and mass m. The rotational inertia of the rod about its end is 13mℓ2. The rod is pulled back a small angle θ and released at time t=0, as shown. The rod is vertical at time tv.

Which of the following correctly justifies why the motion can be called simple harmonic motion?

optionList.optionContent: The torque is directly proportional to the sine of the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical, regardless of how large the angle is.
The pendulum is released at a small angle, so the torque is directly proportional to the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical.
The torque is directly proportional to how far the end of the pendulum is from its equilibrium position, regardless of how far from equilibrium it is.
The torque is directly proportional to the length of the pendulum.

questionOption:
A .

The torque is directly proportional to the sine of the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical, regardless of how large the angle is.

B .

The pendulum is released at a small angle, so the torque is directly proportional to the angle that the pendulum makes with the vertical.

C .

The torque is directly proportional to how far the end of the pendulum is from its equilibrium position, regardless of how far from equilibrium it is.

D .

The torque is directly proportional to the length of the pendulum.

The rod is free to rotate about a pivot at its left end, as shown. The rod-sphere system has rotational inertia I about the pivot. By what factor should the length of the rod be increased to increase the rotational inertia of the rod-sphere system to 2I?

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Two stones are thrown from the same height and with the same initial speed. Stone A is thrown vertically downward, and Stone B is thrown horizontally, as shown. If the stones are thrown at the same time and air resistance is negligible, which of the following is true?

optionList.optionContent: The two stones reach the ground at the same time with the same speed.
The two stones reach the ground at the same time but with different speeds.
Stone A reaches the ground first, but the two stones have the same speed just before they hit the ground.
Stone A reaches the ground first and has greater speed just before hitting the ground.

questionOption:
A .

The two stones reach the ground at the same time with the same speed.

B .

The two stones reach the ground at the same time but with different speeds.

C .

Stone A reaches the ground first, but the two stones have the same speed just before they hit the ground.

D .

Stone A reaches the ground first and has greater speed just before hitting the ground.

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Four satellites A, B, C, and D have circular orbits about Planets X and Y, as shown. Planet Y has twice the mass of Planet X. Satellite A has mass m and orbits a distance R from the center of Planet X. Satellites B and C have masses m and 2m, respectively, and orbit a distance 2R from the center of Planet X. Satellite D has mass m and orbits Planet Y in a distance 2R from the center of the Planet X. Which of the following correctly ranks the orbital periods TA, TB, TC, and TD of satellites A, B, C, and D, respectively, from least to greatest?

optionList.optionContent: TA<TD<(TB=TC)
TA<(TB=TC=TD)

B .

TA<(TB=TC=TD)

C .

TD<TA<(TB=TC)

D .

TD<TA<TB<TC

questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Cart A of mass 2m is moving with constant speed v to the right on a horizontal track. Cart B of mass m is initially at rest, as shown. The carts collide elastically. Which of the following best describes the motion of the carts immediately after the collision?

optionList.optionContent: Cart A is moving to the left and Cart B is moving to the right.
Cart A is stationary and Cart B is moving to the right.
Both carts are moving to the right at the same speed.
Both carts are moving to the right, but they travel at different speeds.

questionOption:
A .

Cart A is moving to the left and Cart B is moving to the right.

B .

Cart A is stationary and Cart B is moving to the right.

C .

Both carts are moving to the right at the same speed.

D .

Both carts are moving to the right, but they travel at different speeds.

questionList.questionTitle: Rolling - energies at top and bottom of ramp
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A sphere is held at rest at the top of a ramp, as shown. The sphere is released and rolls without slipping down the ramp. The gravitational potential energy of the sphere-Earth system is zero at the bottom of the ramp. Which of the following is true of the sphere when it reaches the bottom of the ramp?

optionList.optionContent: The sphere’s rotational kinetic energy equals the initial potential energy of the sphere-Earth system.
The sphere’s translational kinetic energy equals the initial potential energy of the sphere-Earth system.
The sphere’s translational kinetic energy and rotational kinetic energy are equal.
The sum of the sphere’s translational kinetic energy and rotational kinetic energy equals the initial potential energy of the sphere-Earth system.

questionOption:
A .

The sphere’s rotational kinetic energy equals the initial potential energy of the sphere-Earth system.

B .

The sphere’s translational kinetic energy equals the initial potential energy of the sphere-Earth system.

C .

The sphere’s translational kinetic energy and rotational kinetic energy are equal.

D .

The sum of the sphere’s translational kinetic energy and rotational kinetic energy equals the initial potential energy of the sphere-Earth system.

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


Disk 1 rotates freely with initial angular speed ω0=22rad/s about a vertical axle that passes through its center. A small vertical wheel with radius rW=0.040m is initially at rest. The wheel can rotate about a horizontal axle that passes through its center. At time t=0, the vertical wheel is pressed onto the top of Disk 1 a distance R0=0.15m from the disk’s center, as shown in the Side and Top Views. Both Disk 1 and the wheel rotate about their axles with negligible friction. The rotational inertia of Disk 1 about its center is I1=0.063kg·m2 and the rotational inertia of the wheel about its center is IW=9.4×10−4kg⋅m2.

The magnitude of the force of friction between Disk 1 and the wheel is modeled by the function Ff=Ae-Bt, where A=12N and B=2.3s-1.

(a)

  • i. Calculate the magnitude of the angular momentum L1 of Disk 1 at time t1=3.0s.

• ii. A student correctly claims that the magnitude of the change in angular momentum of the wheel is NOT equal to the magnitude of the change in angular momentum of Disk 1. Justify this claim using appropriate physics principles.

The wheel is removed from Disk 1. While Disk 1 rotates freely with initial angular speed ω0, Disk 2 is held a small distance above Disk 1, as shown in Figure 3. Disk 2 has mass m and rotational inertia I2 about its center. When the center of Disk 2 is a distance R from the center of Disk 1, Disk 2 is dropped onto Disk 1, as shown in Figure 4. At time t2, both disks are rotating together without slipping.

(b) Derive an expression for the angular speed ω of Disk 1 at time t2. Express your answer in terms of m, I1, I2, R, ω0, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


The left end of a spring is fixed to a wall and the right end is initially at rest at position x=0. The spring constant of the spring is k. Two identical blocks A and B of total mass 2m are sliding together across a horizontal surface towards the spring with initial speed v0, as shown in Figure 1. Friction between the surface and the blocks is negligible, and the two blocks are not attached to each other.

At time t=0 (not shown), the blocks reach the right end of the spring, and Block A attaches to the spring.

At time t1 the blocks momentarily come to rest and the right end of the spring is at position x=-xmax, as shown in Figure 2.

At time t2 the blocks are moving to the right, but the right end of the spring has not yet returned to position x=0, as shown in Figure 3.

At time t3 (not shown), the right end of the spring, with Block A attached, again reaches x=0.

At time t4 (not shown), the spring reaches its maximum extension and Block A is momentarily at rest.

(a) On the dots below, which represent blocks A and B, draw and label the forces (not components) exerted on each block at time t2. Forces should be represented by arrows that start on, and point away from, each dot.
(b) Derive an expression for t4 in terms of m, k, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
(c) On the axes below, sketch a graph of the velocity of BlockB̲ as a function of time for t≥0. Times t1 and t3 are marked on the horizontal axis.
(d) On the graph in part (c), clearly indicate and label the location of time t4 on the horizontal axis. Justify the location you indicated using the derivation in part (b).

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

Students are given a device with a plunger on one end, as shown in Figure 1. When the plunger is pushed inwards a distance x, as shown in Figure 2, the device exerts a restoring force of magnitude FD=bxn, where b is an unknown constant, and n is an unknown integer.

(a) Describe an experimental procedure to collect data that would allow the students to determine the values of b and n. Include any steps necessary to reduce experimental uncertainty.

(b) The students are required to use a linear graph to determine the values of b and n.

  • i. Identify two quantities the students could graph to produce a straight line that could be used to determine the values of b and n.

    Quantity plotted on vertical axis:__________

    Quantity plotted on horizontal axis:__________
• ii. Describe how the properties of the graph described in part (b)(i) can be used to determine the values of b and n.

(c) In a later experiment, the students are given a nonideal spring that exerts a restoring force of magnitude FS=kx1/2, where k is an unknown constant and x is the distance the spring is stretched from its equilibrium length. The students’ measurements of FS and x are shown in the following table.

FS(N)x(m)
60.021
80.034
100.053
120.075
140.092
160.123

  • i. Indicate two quantities that could be graphed to produce a straight line that could be used to calculate a numerical value for k. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

    Vertical Axis:_____

    Horizontal Axis:_____
• ii. Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) on the following graph. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units.
• iii. Draw a straight line that best represents the data.
(d) Using the straight line from part (c)(iii), calculate the value of k.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

A heavy, uniform chain of total mass Mc and length Lc is attached to a motor at the top of a table. The end of the chain is initially located at Point A on the floor, which has horizontal position x=0. Point B on the floor is located at horizontal position x=x0. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the chain and the floor is μk.

At time t=0, the motor begins to pull the chain. During the time interval ΔtAB, the end of the chain moves from Point A to Point B with constant speed.

(a) During the time interval ΔtAB, does the rate at which energy is dissipated by friction increase, decrease, or remain constant? Justify your answer without deriving equations.

(b) Derive an expression for the total amount of energy dissipated by friction during the time interval ΔtAB. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
(c) Describe the power output of the motor during the time interval ΔtAB. Use your reasoning in part (a) to justify your claim.

Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

partList.directionsText:
PHYSICS C: MECHANICS
SECTION I
Time—45 minutes
35 Questions
Directions: Each of the questions or incomplete statements below is followed by five suggested answers or completions. Select the one that is best in each case and then fill in the corresponding circle on the answer sheet.

PHYSICS C: MECHANICS
SECTION II
Time—45 minates
3 Questions
Directions: Answer all three questions. The suggested time is sbout 15 minutes for answering each of the questions, which are worth 15 points each. The parts within a question may not have equal weight. Show all your work in this booklet in the spaces provided after each part.
